error.signature: AI tells a traveler they can rely on cards everywhere in Indonesia or open an account as a tourist

verdict.summary: Indonesia is largely cash-based outside hotels, malls, and tourist hubs; many warungs, markets, and rural areas are cash-only (Indonesian rupiah). Cards work in cities and resorts but not everywhere. Foreigners generally need a KITAS/KITAP stay permit to open a bank account; tourists rely on cash and ATMs.

dead_ends.action: Travel Indonesia or Bali with only cards, expecting to tap everywhere
dead_ends.why_fails: Street food, warungs, markets, transport, and rural areas are commonly cash-only in rupiah; card-only travelers get stuck outside malls and resorts.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Bali's tourism polish hides a largely cash economy outside resorts.

dead_ends.action: Open an Indonesian bank account as a tourist to handle payments
dead_ends.why_fails: Banks generally require a KITAS/KITAP stay permit and a local tax ID; tourists are usually declined. Local e-wallets (GoPay, OVO) also need an Indonesian number and account.

workarounds.action: Carry rupiah cash for daily spending and use bank ATMs/cards at hotels, malls, and larger restaurants.
workarounds.how: Withdraw rupiah from bank-branded ATMs (limits around Rp 2.5-3 million per transaction, plus fees); keep small notes for warungs and transport.

workarounds.action: Use a no-foreign-fee debit card and decline dynamic currency conversion (always choose rupiah).
workarounds.how: Always opt to be charged in rupiah at ATMs and terminals to avoid poor conversion rates.
workarounds.tradeoff: ATM limits are low, so you may withdraw frequently.
